The outbound check confirmed #TK-8814; the jeweler received #TK-8815 — Stellan swapped bands during verification and resealed with the next one in his own pack. Clue 2 shows the preparation: calcite ordered to match uncut beryl's visual profile arrived from a Dutch supplier twelve days before the appointment — the substitution materials were sourced before the job was contracted. Clue 3 surfaces the pattern: three of Stellan's four prior verifications ended in the same one-serial band discrepancy and disputed contents, establishing this as a practiced method, not a one-off. Dex's post-7 PM arrival, Lin's 5 PM clock-out, Marta's sealed cargo log, and Bev's 6:02 PM departure eliminate every other suspect. Stellan replaced the stones during verification, resealed the crate with the next band from his own supply, and the depot's outbound check certified a switch he had already made.

title: The Proof Sheet
case: An original copper printing plate engraved by a 19th-century master printmaker — valued at $41,000 — disappeared from the secure archive at Holloway Print Studio. The plate was logged out for an authorized session at 10 AM and returned by the curator at 11:15 AM; it was found missing during the 4 PM archive audit.

suspects.name: Priya
suspects.alibi: I supervised the session and personally locked the plate back into the archive at 11:15 AM. I have the only key.

suspects.name: Corwin
suspects.alibi: I ran exactly twelve prints as authorized, then handed the plate to Priya and watched her lock it up. I left the building at 11:20.

suspects.name: Brin
suspects.alibi: I was preparing inks and paper stock on the studio floor all morning. I've never been inside the archive — I don't have access.

suspects.name: Kieran
suspects.alibi: I was dealing with a water pressure issue on the second floor most of the day. I don't have archive access.

suspects.name: Solange
suspects.role: Gallery Owner, Visiting to Inspect the Print Run
suspects.alibi: I observed the session from the glass-partitioned gallery area. I left at 11:30 — Brin walked me out.

starter: The archive is a climate-controlled room with a keypad entry log. Entries for the day: Priya (10:02 AM in, 10:05 AM out — plate retrieval), Priya and Corwin (11:12 AM in, 11:15 AM out — plate return), Priya alone (1:43 PM in, 1:51 PM out). The 1:43 PM entry has no corresponding work order. A review of Priya's access history shows this was her third unscheduled solo archive visit in four weeks, each occurring on a day when a high-value plate was used in an authorized session.

The studio's outgoing parcel log — maintained for insurance tracking — records a courier package labeled 'conservation materials' prepared at the shipping station at 1:55 PM, four minutes after Priya's 1:51 PM archive exit. The package dimensions — 38 by 28 by 4 centimeters — correspond precisely to the plate's documented measurements. Priya is the only staff member authorized to prepare outgoing archival parcels.

Priya's personal email, reviewed under warrant, contains a thread begun eleven days prior with a private buyer in Rotterdam. The buyer offered €28,000 for the specific plate — citing 'provenance documents already in hand,' a detail that requires intimate prior knowledge of the studio's holdings. Priya's reply reads: 'Confirmed. Will arrange transfer on next authorized session day.'

exonerations.corwin: Corwin departed at 11:20 AM, confirmed by the building entry system. He does not have archive keypad credentials; the log shows no entry attempt by his badge at any point during the day.
exonerations.brin: Brin has no archive keypad access and was assigned to the ground-floor ink station, visible on studio cameras throughout the morning. The archive is on a separate floor accessible only by key-coded stairwell.
exonerations.kieran: Kieran was assigned to the second-floor mechanical room from 9 AM to 3:30 PM. His badge log shows no access attempt on the archive level, and his work order is co-signed by the building engineer.
exonerations.solange: Solange observed from the glass-partitioned gallery and was escorted out by Brin at 11:30 AM. The building entry system shows her visitor badge deactivated at 11:34 AM; she never accessed the archive floor.

reveal: Clue 1 identifies Priya's 1:43 PM archive entry as the critical event — unscheduled, unlogged against any work order, and the third such unexplained visit in a month, each tied to an authorized session day when a high-value plate would be out of permanent storage and in her hands. Clue 2 closes the physical chain: four minutes after her 1:51 PM exit, a courier parcel matching the plate's exact dimensions was prepared at the shipping station by Priya, the only person authorized to do so. Clue 3 supplies both motive and premeditation: eleven days before the session, she had already confirmed the sale via email, promising delivery on 'next authorized session day' — meaning the session itself was simply the occasion she had been waiting for. Corwin's 11:20 AM departure and absent archive credentials, Brin's restricted floor access, Kieran's mechanical room work order, and Solange's visitor badge deactivation eliminate every other suspect. Priya used the authorized session as cover, returned alone at 1:43 PM, and had the plate in a courier parcel bound for Rotterdam by 1:55 PM.

title: The Blind Vintage
case: Two bottles of 1945 Romanée-Conti — each valued at $28,000 — disappeared from the temperature-controlled vault at the Versant Private Dining Club. The bottles were confirmed present during a 2 PM cellar audit and found missing at 5:45 PM.

suspects.name: Conrad
suspects.alibi: I conducted the 2 PM audit myself and staged three bottles in the tasting anteroom for the appraiser. I left the anteroom at 3:07 and didn't return until nearly 6 PM.

suspects.name: Yeva
suspects.alibi: I organized inventory in the cellar this morning until 11 AM. I haven't been back since.

suspects.name: Rupert
suspects.alibi: Conrad brought three bottles to the anteroom for me. I examined them, wrote my report, and left at 3:56. I never went into the main vault.

suspects.name: Desta
suspects.alibi: A party of eight dined this evening. I was managing service the entire time and wasn't near the cellar.

suspects.name: Fabrice
suspects.role: Refrigeration Technician, Called for a Temperature Alarm
suspects.alibi: I serviced the vault control panel between 4:30 and 5:30 PM. It was a sensor glitch — the temperature was fine. I worked in the utility corridor the whole time.

starter: The cellar key card log records Conrad (2:00–2:14 PM audit), Conrad (2:58–3:03 PM staging), and Conrad swiping Rupert into the tasting anteroom at 3:03 PM, with Conrad exiting at 3:07 PM. Rupert's badge does not grant independent vault access. However, the vault's interior motion sensor — a separate layer from the key card system — recorded movement inside the main vault at 3:41 PM, lasting four minutes. The vault door opens from the anteroom side via a push-latch requiring no card swipe; a card swipe is required only for entry from the corridor. Conrad did not re-enter until 5:45 PM.

Rupert's written appraisal report is time-stamped 3:52 PM, yet the anteroom camera shows him departing at 3:56 PM — four minutes after the vault motion ended at 3:45. A search of Rupert's evaluation case found a custom foam-lined carrier insert with two cylindrical cavities sized to Bordeaux-format bottles. The insert is not standard equipment for his appraisal firm; no equivalent insert appears in any other evaluator's kit at the firm.

A private auction house in Zurich received a consignment inquiry at 6:22 PM that evening listing 'two bottles, 1945 DRC, provenance documentation to follow.' The inquiry was sent from an email account registered to Rupert's appraisal firm. The Versant Club had never authorized those bottles for sale or consignment. The price requested was €48,000 per bottle.

exonerations.conrad: Conrad's last vault entry was at 3:07 PM when he staged the bottles and left Rupert in the anteroom. The key card log confirms he did not re-enter until 5:45 PM. As the sole key card holder, Conrad had no need to use the push-latch workaround available only from the anteroom side.
exonerations.yeva: Yeva's last cellar access was at 11:04 AM, confirmed by the key card log. Kitchen cameras place her continuously at the mise en place station from noon onward, corroborated by three kitchen staff members.
exonerations.desta: Desta does not hold cellar key card access. The service camera shows her at the front-of-house station from 4:30 to 8 PM with no gap exceeding three minutes.
exonerations.fabrice: Fabrice worked on the external control panel in the utility corridor and never entered the vault interior. His work order specifies panel access only, and his badge was never swiped at the vault door.

reveal: Clue 1 establishes the mechanism: Conrad swiped Rupert into the anteroom at 3:03 PM and exited at 3:07. From the anteroom, the vault door opens via push-latch with no card swipe required — an entry point invisible to the key card log. The motion sensor caught what the log could not: movement inside the main vault at 3:41 PM, with Rupert the only person in the anteroom during that window. Clue 2 confirms he was there and prepared: the vault motion ran for four minutes ending at 3:45, and Rupert's case contained a purpose-built dual-bottle carrier with no legitimate appraisal use. Clue 3 shows the timeline compressed to hours: by 6:22 PM, Rupert's firm email was pitching two 1945 DRC bottles to a Zurich auction house for €48,000 each — bottles the club had never put up for sale. Conrad's 3:07 exit and 5:45 re-entry, Yeva's 11:04 AM last access, Desta's unbroken service camera record, and Fabrice's corridor-only work order eliminate every other suspect. Rupert accessed the vault through the anteroom's unlogged push-latch at 3:41 PM, removed the two bottles with a pre-fitted carrier, and was already seeking buyers before the club had finished dinner service.

title: The Soft Close
case: A signed first-edition copy of The Great Gatsby (1925), inscribed by F. Scott Fitzgerald to his editor Maxwell Perkins and valued at $88,000, was found to have been replaced with a high-quality facsimile after lot 47 sold at Westhaven Auction House for $82,000. The substitution was discovered when the buyer's authenticator examined the book post-sale.

suspects.name: Ingrid
suspects.alibi: I catalogued and photographed every lot two days before the sale. The book was genuine when I handled it.

suspects.name: Vance
suspects.alibi: I ran lot 47 from the podium. I read from the catalogue — I never physically handled the book.

suspects.name: Cassia
suspects.alibi: The book was in a locked acrylic case for the entire two-day preview. I supervised the floor but never handled the piece directly.

suspects.name: Noel
suspects.alibi: I dropped off the book on Monday and didn't return until sale day. I didn't handle it after intake.

suspects.name: Petra
suspects.alibi: I carried lot 47 from the secure room to the podium and back — both times gloved and directly watched by the auctioneer.

starter: The lot was photographed and catalogued by Ingrid two days before the sale. The catalogue photographs — published on the auction house's public website — include a clear image of Fitzgerald's title-page inscription. An Edinburgh bookseller who produces period-accurate facsimiles received a commission order the morning after those photographs were posted online. The order specified 'title-page inscription matching Westhaven lot 47' and was placed from an email address registered to a disposable domain created eleven days before the catalogue went live.

The facsimile was printed on paper with a lignin-content profile consistent with genuine 1920s stock — a technically precise specification that requires conservation-level expertise to identify and source correctly. Ingrid's employment history includes three years at a rare documents conservation laboratory where lignin-content analysis was a core service. The Edinburgh bookseller confirmed the commission brief included a technical note specifying '1920s lignin-matched paper, acid-free, minimum 85 gsm' — language that goes well beyond what a casual buyer would know to request.

The lot storage room camera was reported as malfunctioning at 3:14 PM on the evening before the sale — the only camera outage in the building that week. The IT maintenance log shows the fault was reported from an internal extension registered to the registrar's office. Ingrid's access badge log shows she entered the lot storage room alone at 6:42 PM that same evening, outside her cataloguing duties and after normal business hours. The camera came back online the following morning, thirty minutes before the auction opened.

exonerations.vance: Vance conducted the entire auction from the podium in view of forty attendees and a sale-day camera recording. He did not physically handle the book at any point.
exonerations.cassia: The preview display used a locked acrylic case; the case key is held by the security desk, not the preview coordinator. Cassia does not have lot storage room access after hours, and the swap required unwitnessed handling during the storage period, not during public preview.
exonerations.noel: Noel's consignor badge was deactivated after Monday's intake per standard protocol. The lot storage room log shows no entry by his credentials after that date, and the auction house's intake records confirm the book was genuine at drop-off — witnessed by Ingrid herself.
exonerations.petra: Petra's role begins only on sale day, after the book had already been in storage for two days. The facsimile was in the lot before Petra collected it from the secure room; her handling was entirely post-substitution and conducted in full view of the auctioneer and sale-floor cameras.

reveal: Clue 1 establishes the sequence: Ingrid photographed and posted the catalogue images, and within hours a commission order for a facsimile replicating the specific inscription appeared at an Edinburgh bookseller — placed from a disposable domain registered eleven days before the catalogue went live, indicating the plan preceded the public posting. Clue 2 supplies the expertise fingerprint: the facsimile's lignin-matched paper specification requires the kind of conservation laboratory knowledge Ingrid accumulated over three years — knowledge that narrows the pool of possible authors to a very small group, and that she possessed directly. Clue 3 closes the physical access chain: the storage room camera she reported as faulty went dark at 3:14 PM, Ingrid entered the room alone at 6:42 PM under no work order, and the camera returned online the next morning just before the sale. Vance's podium-only role, Cassia's post-hours access restriction, Noel's deactivated badge, and Petra's sale-day-only handling all eliminate the remaining suspects. Ingrid photographed the inscription, briefed the facsimile maker, killed the only witness camera, and made the swap the night before the auction.

title: The Green Wall
case: Three specimens of Amorphophallus titanum cultivated from a rare tissue-culture program — collectively valued at $31,000 — were removed from the research greenhouse at Aldersham Botanical Garden and replaced with ornamental plants. The specimens were confirmed present at 7:28 AM; the substitution was discovered during an 11:30 AM inspection.

suspects.name: Wren
suspects.alibi: I ran the 7 AM inventory, confirmed all three specimens, locked the greenhouse at 7:28, and went straight to a grant review meeting.

suspects.name: Lachlan
suspects.alibi: I was watering the standard collections in the adjacent greenhouse from 8 to 10 AM. I don't have a key to the research greenhouse.

suspects.name: Simone
suspects.alibi: I had temporary keycard access to conduct root structure observations. I was in the research greenhouse from 9 to 10:30 AM and followed all protocols.

suspects.name: Harper
suspects.alibi: I photographed the public greenhouse and the propagation lab. I wasn't given research greenhouse access.

suspects.name: Morwenna
suspects.alibi: I did a building systems check in the research greenhouse mechanical room at 8:15 AM — a separate utility entrance. I was in and out in fifteen minutes.

starter: The research greenhouse uses a keycard entry log. Entries: Wren (7:02 AM open, 7:28 AM close/lock), Morwenna (8:15–8:31 AM — mechanical room via separate interior utility door), Simone (9:03–10:34 AM). The three specimens were confirmed present at Wren's 7:28 AM exit and missing at 11:30 AM, narrowing the theft to Simone's session. The ornamental Dracaena trifasciata plants found in their place were potted in the research containers within the preceding 48 hours, based on soil moisture gradient analysis — meaning they were prepared before the visit, not sourced on site.

Simone's access application submitted three weeks prior requested observation rights for Amorphophallus root structure. However, the tissue-culture program's external research coordinator confirmed no corresponding study was registered at Simone's home institution. A check with that institution's botany department found Simone had been on personal leave at the time the application was submitted, meaning she used her institutional credentials to obtain access with no active departmental sponsorship.

A rare-plant broker in Singapore received a consignment inquiry two days after the theft listing 'three juvenile A. titanum specimens, tissue-culture provenance.' The inquiry was sent from a personal email account, but the sender's username matched a handle associated with Simone's public profile on a rare-plant enthusiast forum, where she had posted about A. titanum cultivation fourteen months earlier. Customs records show a refrigerated shipment declared as 'ornamental foliage' departed a courier hub near Aldersham within 36 hours of the theft.

exonerations.wren: Wren's 7:28 AM exit predates the theft window by over 90 minutes. The keycard log shows no re-entry, and her grant review meeting attendance was confirmed by three faculty members from 8 AM to 11:15 AM.
exonerations.lachlan: Lachlan's keycard does not grant research greenhouse access; the entry log shows no attempt on the research greenhouse door from his badge. His watering work in the adjacent greenhouse was confirmed by shift logs and his supervisor.
exonerations.harper: Harper's visitor lanyard was programmed for the public greenhouse and propagation lab only. The access log shows a rejected entry attempt on the research greenhouse door from Harper's badge at 9:47 AM.
exonerations.morwenna: Morwenna entered only the mechanical room via a separate utility entrance that does not connect to the research floor. The utility-access sensor confirms she did not cross into the research area during her 8:15–8:31 AM visit.

reveal: Clue 1 narrows the theft to Simone's 9:03–10:34 AM session as the only authorized access after Wren's confirmed 7:28 AM departure, and the recently potted Dracaena replacements — staged within 48 hours based on soil moisture — prove the substitution was arranged before the visit, not improvised. Clue 2 dismantles Simone's legitimacy entirely: she obtained greenhouse access using institutional credentials while on personal leave, with no registered study at her home department — meaning the research application was a fabrication designed solely to secure entry. Clue 3 traces the specimens forward: a Singapore broker received a consignment inquiry for three tissue-culture A. titanum specimens within two days, sent from a username matching Simone's established forum identity, and a refrigerated 'ornamental foliage' shipment departed a local courier hub within 36 hours. Wren's 7:28 AM exit and confirmed meeting, Lachlan's restricted badge, Harper's access denial log, and Morwenna's utility-only entrance eliminate every other suspect. Simone fabricated a research application, arrived with pre-staged replacement plants, removed the specimens during her authorized session, and had them in transit to Singapore before the theft was even discovered.

title: The Long Game
case: A 1980 Olympic Gold Medal — owned by retired sprinter Elliot Marsh and kept in a display case in the Members' Gallery at Fenwick Athletic Club — disappeared during an invitational dinner event. The medal was confirmed present at 6 PM; it was found missing at 10:30 PM.

suspects.name: Tilda
suspects.alibi: I did the 6 PM walkthrough and locked the case myself. I was at the reception desk the rest of the evening.

suspects.name: Rowan
suspects.alibi: I was managing the event floor all evening. I had no reason to go anywhere near the gallery.

suspects.name: Elliot
suspects.role: Medal Owner and Club Patron, Guest of Honor
suspects.alibi: It's my medal and I put it there twelve years ago. I was at the head table all night — I wouldn't steal my own property.

suspects.name: Zara
suspects.alibi: My team serviced the dining room only. I was in the kitchen or at the service pass the entire evening.

suspects.name: Vann
suspects.role: AV Technician, Hired for the Event
suspects.alibi: I was managing the AV system. My cables run through the building, including near the gallery corridor, but I was at the AV desk all night.

starter: The gallery display case has a contact-open sensor. Log: Tilda (6:02 PM close/lock), case opened at 8:47 PM, case closed and left unlocked at 8:51 PM. The gallery corridor has a single camera covering its entrance from the main hall. Footage from 8:44 to 8:53 PM shows Vann entering the corridor at 8:44 PM carrying a cable bundle, his body partially occluding the frame until 8:52 PM. The corridor's secondary motion zone — the area adjacent to the display case — registered movement at 8:46 PM, two minutes after his entry.

A post-event inventory of Vann's equipment confirmed all cables and hardware were accounted for at the AV station — none had been routed through the gallery corridor. The venue's cable schematic, which was emailed to Vann five days before the event, shows no AV routing requirement in that corridor. Vann's stated reason for being there is contradicted by his own pre-event documentation. A physical search of his equipment bag found a key with a warded profile matching the display case lock — not a standard key type and with no legitimate purpose for an AV technician.

Eighteen months prior, Vann had worked an AV contract at the Hartfield Memorabilia Auction House. On the night of that event, a signed Jesse Owens photograph disappeared from a display corridor under unresolved circumstances. Hartfield's corridor camera from that evening shows a figure in an AV lanyard entering the display corridor during setup; event staffing records identify Vann as the AV technician on that contract. A sports memorabilia dealer in Antwerp had at the time received an inquiry about Jesse Owens ephemera from an account that subsequently went dormant — and reopened five weeks before the Fenwick dinner with a new inquiry specifically about pre-1984 Olympic track-and-field medals.

exonerations.tilda: Tilda locked the case at 6:02 PM and was confirmed at the reception desk from 6:15 PM onward by three event staff and the door entry log showing her processing arriving guests. Her badge shows no archive or gallery access after 6:02 PM.
exonerations.rowan: Rowan was on the event floor throughout the evening. Four time-stamped radio check-ins with catering staff between 7 PM and 9:30 PM place her in the dining area continuously during the 8:44–8:53 PM theft window.
exonerations.elliot: Elliot was the guest of honor seated at the head table. The dining room camera places him there continuously from the 7 PM seating through the 10 PM speeches. He does not hold a key to the gallery case.
exonerations.zara: Zara's team was restricted to the dining room and kitchen service corridor. Catering staff confirm Zara was in the kitchen during the 8:44–8:53 PM window, corroborated by her sous chef and two waitstaff.

reveal: Clue 1 places Vann in the gallery corridor at 8:44 PM — the exact window of the 8:47 PM case-open alert — and the secondary motion sensor registers him near the display case by 8:46. He was the only person recorded in that corridor during the theft window. Clue 2 removes any innocent explanation: no cable routing required that corridor, his own pre-event schematic proves it, and a key matching the case's warded lock profile was found in his bag — equipment with no legitimate use for an AV technician, brought specifically for this purpose. Clue 3 surfaces the pattern and the buyer: an identical approach at Hartfield Auction House eighteen months prior — a solitary AV technician in a display corridor, a missing piece of Olympic-era memorabilia, an Antwerp dealer inquiry — and the same dormant account reactivated five weeks before the Fenwick event asking specifically about pre-1984 Olympic medals. Tilda's desk confirmation, Rowan's radio logs, Elliot's dining room camera, and Zara's kitchen staff all clear the remaining suspects. Vann came prepared with a purpose-cut key, entered the corridor under cover of a fabricated cable errand, and had already reconnected with his buyer before the evening was over.

title: The Last Call
case: At 6:47 PM in the Aldwich Station first-class departure lounge, a leather attaché case containing signed transfer documents for a private art acquisition went missing from table 7. The owner had stepped out for a two-minute phone call at 6:44 PM and found it absent when he returned at 6:52. Staff with lounge access and a single registered guest had been in the building during the window.

suspects.name: Fletcher
suspects.alibi: I was at the check-in desk from 6:35 onward without interruption. I cleared an empty cup from table 7 around 6:40, before the owner even arrived, but I never went back out. The camera will show me the whole time.

suspects.name: Odette
suspects.alibi: I brought the 6:45 evening tray to table 7 — chamomile, a pastry, and a newspaper from the lounge rack. I set it down and went straight back to the kitchen.

suspects.name: Graham
suspects.alibi: I was moving bags for the 7:05 northbound group. I pushed my cart through the lounge around 6:50 to reach the platform elevator — I didn't stop for a second. I was running behind.

suspects.name: Madeleine
suspects.alibi: I'd been at table 4 since 6:28, reading. I noticed the attaché case at table 7 when I sat down — you couldn't miss it. I wasn't anywhere near that table.

suspects.name: Corin
suspects.alibi: I was in the service corridor fixing the ceiling fan unit. That's accessed from the corridor side — I never set foot in the lounge itself the entire shift.

starter: The lounge has a single entry camera covering the doorway from the main hall. Between 6:40 and 7:00 PM: Fletcher is at the check-in desk without leaving; Madeleine is at table 4 and never approaches table 7; Graham enters at 6:49 and exits at 6:52, his cart tracked along the far wall, never within 4 meters of table 7; Odette enters at 6:44 and exits at 6:47. The attaché case is visible on table 7 in the 6:44 frame; it is absent in the 6:52 frame.

The café's kitchen delivery log records Odette's 6:45 tray for table 7 as: one chamomile pot, one pastry, one newspaper. The lounge's newspaper circulation clipboard — where guests sign out papers from the rack — shows no newspaper removed between 6:00 PM and 7:30 PM. The newspaper Odette says she took from the rack was never removed from it.

The service corridor connects the café kitchen to the lounge's back utility closet and to a side exit that bypasses the platform camera zone. A chamomile pot lid matching the café's crockery pattern was found in the side-exit stairwell three floors below the lounge. Odette's end-of-shift bag, examined by station security, contained a broadsheet newspaper folded around the signed art transfer documents.

exonerations.fletcher: The camera shows Fletcher at the check-in desk from 6:35 through 7:10 without any gap. His position is 14 meters from table 7, and coverage is continuous — no interval places him near the attaché case.
exonerations.graham: Graham's entry at 6:49 and exit at 6:52 are captured on the same camera. His cart path follows the far wall and keeps him a minimum of 4 meters from table 7 throughout. He does not stop or deviate at any point.
exonerations.madeleine: Madeleine is fixed at table 4 from 6:28 until 7:03, confirmed by continuous camera coverage. She has no access to the service corridor, and the camera confirms she never approached table 7 during the entire window.
exonerations.corin: Maintenance access logs show Corin signed in to the fan repair at 6:10 and out at 7:25. The fan unit is at the north end of the service corridor; the side exit where the chamomile lid was found is at the south end. The corridor's internal motion sensor shows no activity at the south end during his shift.

reveal: Clue 1 establishes Odette as the only person near table 7 during the 6:44–6:47 window — and the case disappears within that exact interval. Graham's tracked cart path, Madeleine's fixed seat, and Fletcher's unbroken desk presence keep every other suspect away. Clue 2 catches the lie she built into her own account: she claimed to have taken a newspaper from the lounge rack, but the rack clipboard shows none were removed that evening. She carried no newspaper to the table — she used the tray as cover to lift the attaché case, then exited through the service corridor rather than the main door. Clue 3 traces the route: the chamomile pot lid dropped in the stairwell marks her path to the bypass exit, and the documents found folded inside her newspaper confirm where the case contents ended up. She entered on a legitimate errand and left with the papers concealed beneath her arm.

title: The Reserve List
case: At Calloway Estate Winery during a Saturday afternoon private tasting event, a bottle of 1998 Calloway Reserve — one of only eleven remaining — went missing from the locked reserve cellar between 2:15 PM and 3:40 PM. The cellar key hangs on a hook in the winery office, and only staff and one authorized guest had access to the office during the event.

suspects.name: Fiona
suspects.alibi: I was running the event floor from the moment we opened. I handed out every glass, managed the pour schedule. I stepped out to use the bathroom around 2:30 — five minutes at most.

suspects.name: Nate
suspects.alibi: I was racking barrels in the production cellar from 1:30 to 4 PM. Someone has to do the actual work while everyone sips upstairs. I wasn't anywhere near the reserve cellar.

suspects.name: Priscilla
suspects.alibi: I was running the guided tasting at the barrel table from 2:00 to 3:30. Twelve paying guests had eyes on me the whole time. I don't even hold a key — Fiona manages event-day access.

suspects.name: Aldous
suspects.alibi: I had a pre-event meeting with the owner in the office — we went over the allocation list together. I left the office at 2:05 and stayed in the tasting room until 3:45.

suspects.name: Renata
suspects.alibi: I was setting up and breaking down the food stations all afternoon. In and out of the kitchen next door, but I never had any reason to go to the office or the cellar.

starter: The winery office has a camera covering the key hook and the entrance. The cellar key is visible on the hook at 2:03 PM when the owner leaves the office. Aldous follows the owner out at 2:05 PM — in the final frame of his exit, the key hook is empty. The key does not reappear on the hook until 4:12 PM.

The reserve cellar uses a paper sign-in ledger inside the door, completed only when someone enters from inside after unlocking from outside. The last authorized entry before the event was Friday at 5:50 PM by Nate. The next entry is Saturday at 3:40 PM by Fiona investigating the missing bottle. There is no entry for any access between those two times — but someone with a key can unlock the door from outside and close it behind them, leaving no ledger record.

Aldous's car was parked in the overflow lot 30 meters from the winery's secondary exterior door — the door opening onto the path leading to the reserve cellar entrance. The estate's exterior motion sensor log shows activity at the secondary door at 2:11 PM and again at 2:27 PM. The tasting room, where Aldous claims to have been from 2:05 onward, is at the opposite end of the building from that door.

exonerations.fiona: Fiona is documented in the tasting room from opening onward in timestamped event photographs showing her handing glasses and speaking with guests. Her bathroom absence at approximately 2:30 is corroborated by a guest who asked her a question on her return, and her route takes her nowhere near the office or cellar.
exonerations.nate: The production cellar has its own entry log. Nate signed in at 1:32 PM and the cellar's internal sensor log shows continuous barrel-work activity through the 2:00–4:00 PM window. The production and reserve cellars are on opposite sides of the winery building.
exonerations.priscilla: Priscilla led the guided tasting at the barrel table in full view of twelve guests from 2:00 to 3:30 PM. Three guests independently corroborated her continuous presence. She does not hold a key to the reserve cellar.
exonerations.renata: Catering sign-in manifests place Renata at delivery check-in at 1:15 PM and breakdown sign-out at 3:55 PM. Kitchen security footage shows her in the kitchen or adjacent service area throughout the event. She has no key access to the office.

reveal: The office camera is unambiguous: the cellar key was on the hook when the owner left at 2:03, and gone when Aldous exited two minutes later. He was the sole person in the office during that gap. Clue 2 explains the clean ledger — the key allows access without any inside entry, which is why there is no record between Friday and Fiona's Saturday discovery. The theft was designed to leave no paper trail. Clue 3 physically tracks the route: the secondary exterior door motion at 2:11 and 2:27 maps the outbound and return legs of a trip to the reserve cellar entrance, at a time Aldous claims to have been in the tasting room on the opposite side of the building. Fiona's guest interactions, Nate's sensor log, Priscilla's twelve witnesses, and Renata's kitchen footage account entirely for the remaining suspects.

title: The Master Take
case: At Threadneedle Sound, a private recording studio, the sole backup drive containing the master recordings for Yolanda Vex's debut album was taken from the studio's lock-up cabinet between 9:15 PM and 10:00 PM on a Tuesday session night. The cabinet key is part of the house key ring kept at the front desk.

suspects.name: Yolanda
suspects.alibi: I was in the live room laying down the last track until about 9:30. Then I went to the artist lounge to call my manager. I didn't go anywhere near the front desk or the lock-up cabinet.

suspects.name: Nico
suspects.alibi: I was at the console in the control room the whole session. I stepped out around 9:20 to grab a coffee from the kitchen — three minutes, maybe four. That's it.

suspects.name: Daria
suspects.alibi: I was at the front desk all evening — I'm the one who holds the key ring. I went to the bathroom at 9:25 and came back at 9:30.

suspects.name: Lester
suspects.alibi: I finished my overdubs by 9 PM, packed up in the green room, and left at 9:10. I signed out on the sheet and everything. Check the log.

suspects.name: Petra
suspects.alibi: I was in the observation lounge — the glass booth above the control room — listening to playback with headphones. I had notes on one of the tracks and was writing them up the whole time.

starter: The front desk camera covers the key ring hook and the entrance. At 9:15 PM the key ring is on the hook. At 9:20 PM, Nico passes the desk heading toward the kitchen — the ring is still on the hook. At 9:22 PM, Nico passes the desk returning — the key ring is gone. At 9:29 PM, the key ring reappears on the hook in the frame immediately before Daria returns from the bathroom. Nico is visible entering the control room on the hallway camera at 9:24 PM.

The studio's lock-up cabinet sits in a short corridor between the control room and the kitchen — the exact route Nico used for his coffee run. The kitchen's automatic motion sensor light switched on at 9:23:40. Nico is captured on the internal hallway camera entering the control room at 9:24:15 — 35 seconds from kitchen to control room at a normal walking pace. That gap is consistent with a 10-second detour at the lock-up corridor mid-route.

At 9:47 PM — after the session ended — a device registered to Nico connected to the studio's guest WiFi network. A subpoenaed email sent from that device at 9:47 PM was addressed to a booking coordinator at Vertex Sound, a competing label that had made an unsolicited offer for Yolanda's masters earlier that day. The message read: 'Package secured. Ready for handoff Thursday.'

exonerations.yolanda: Yolanda was in the live room until 9:30 PM, confirmed by the talkback microphone log showing active recording signal until 9:29. The hallway camera captures her moving to the artist lounge at 9:31. She does not pass the front desk or enter the lock-up corridor at any point.
exonerations.daria: Daria left the front desk at 9:25 PM — after the key ring had already been removed. The key ring vanishes from the hook between 9:20 and 9:22, while Daria is still seated at the desk. The bathroom she uses is off the artist lounge corridor, the opposite end of the building from the lock-up cabinet.
exonerations.lester: The sign-out sheet shows Lester's exit at 9:10 PM, and the entrance camera confirms his departure at 9:09. He was not in the building at any point during the theft window.
exonerations.petra: The observation lounge has a dedicated entrance from the upper landing and no direct access to the ground-floor front desk or lock-up corridor. The lounge's internal camera shows Petra seated with headphones from 8:55 PM through 10:05 PM without leaving.

reveal: The front desk camera draws the line: the key ring was present at 9:20 when Nico passed outbound and gone at 9:22 when he passed inbound — no one else touched it. It reappeared at 9:29, five minutes before Daria returned, meaning Nico replaced it on his own initiative. Clue 2 puts the timing in the cabinet corridor: the kitchen light switching on at 9:23:40 and Nico appearing on the hallway camera at 9:24:15 creates a 35-second gap — exactly consistent with a brief stop at the lock-up cabinet on the route between the two rooms. Clue 3 supplies the motive and the buyer: a same-night email from his device to Vertex Sound confirming a handoff, sent within minutes of the session ending. Yolanda's talkback log, Daria's desk timeline, Lester's signed exit, and Petra's lounge camera place every other suspect either outside the building or far from the lock-up corridor.

title: The Deep Tank
case: At the Meridian Aquarium, a hand-carved jade figurine on loan from the Seong Collection for the Pacific Cultures exhibition was removed from its case in the East Wing gallery between closing time (6:00 PM) and the start of the night security sweep (7:30 PM). The building's fob access system logs every after-hours entry by zone.

suspects.name: Elspeth
suspects.alibi: I stayed late to finish installation notes for a new West Wing display. I was at my desk in the curator's office until about 7:15, then left. I didn't go near the East Wing.

suspects.name: Tobias
suspects.alibi: I did my 7:30 PM sweep exactly on schedule — that's when I found the case open. Before that I was at the security desk reviewing yesterday's footage, which is standard protocol before a shift.

suspects.name: Cass
suspects.alibi: I got a pH alert on my phone for the main Pacific tank and came in for an emergency water test. I was at the tank from 6:10 to 6:55 PM. Didn't go anywhere else.

suspects.name: Hendrick
suspects.alibi: I was replacing the HVAC filter in the East Wing mechanical room. It's right next to the gallery, but I do that filter twice a month — completely routine.

suspects.name: Vivienne
suspects.alibi: I had a donor call until 6:30 from my office, then packed up and left immediately. I exited straight through the administrative corridor — I never went near the East Wing.

starter: The fob access system logs every zone entry with a timestamp. Between 6:00 PM and 7:30 PM: Elspeth enters the curator's office zone at 6:02 and exits at 7:17; Cass enters the Pacific tank service annex at 6:08 and exits at 6:57; Vivienne exits the administrative corridor zone at 6:33; Tobias enters the security desk zone at 6:55. Hendrick's fob shows a valid entry into the East Wing mechanical room at 6:05, and a second swipe at 6:41 — an exit read from the East Wing gallery display zone, which is a separate fob area from the mechanical room.

The East Wing display case uses a magnetic latch on a low-power DC circuit. The aquarium's maintenance log shows the display case circuit listed under 'routine inspection' on Hendrick's work order for the evening, despite no prior fault being logged. The same work order lists only the HVAC filter replacement for the mechanical room. The display case entry was added to the work order at 5:58 PM — two minutes before public closing — from a facilities tablet registered to Hendrick.

The figurine's jade base carried a small adhesive conservation tag applied by the Seong Collection conservator at the time of loan. A partial fragment matching that tag's paper stock and adhesive was found adhering to the interior of the East Wing mechanical room's secondary storage cabinet — the one used for personal items and portable tools during a shift. The cabinet had been opened with Hendrick's keyed maintenance pass.

exonerations.elspeth: The curator's office fob log places Elspeth inside from 6:02 to 7:17 without interruption. The office is in the West Wing, 90 meters from the East Wing gallery. Her swipe record shows no East Wing zone entry at any point during the evening.
exonerations.tobias: Tobias's first after-hours swipe is into the security desk zone at 6:55 PM. The security desk camera confirms him at the review terminal from 6:55 until his 7:30 corridor patrol. His East Wing entry at 7:30 was the discovery sweep.
exonerations.cass: Cass was in the Pacific tank service annex — a separate building wing from the East Wing — from 6:08 to 6:57, confirmed by both fob and the tank monitoring system's automatic log of her pH test inputs during that period. The main entrance log shows her departure at 7:03.
exonerations.vivienne: Vivienne's final fob swipe is an exit from the administrative corridor at 6:33. The main entrance camera confirms she left the building at 6:34. She had no East Wing fob access registered for the evening.

reveal: The fob log provides the decisive evidence: Hendrick's 6:41 swipe is an exit read from the East Wing gallery display zone — a zone he had no assigned work in that evening, and a zone that requires passing the display case. He was the only person whose access record places him in the gallery area during the theft window. Clue 2 establishes that the case circuit entry on his work order was added two minutes before closing from his own tablet — a manufactured cover for his presence near the case, added to a work order that already listed only the mechanical room filter. Clue 3 closes the physical chain: the conservation tag fragment inside his personal storage cabinet connects the figurine directly to his possession during his shift. Elspeth's West Wing location, Tobias's desk footage, Cass's tank monitoring log, and Vivienne's building exit remove every other suspect from the gallery zone.

title: The Last Fitting
case: At Brennan & Sons, a bespoke tailor on Cartwright Street, a completed dinner jacket valued at £4,200 and awaiting collection by client Lord Ashworth was taken from the finished-orders rack in the back room between 11:30 AM and 1:45 PM on a Wednesday. The back room is accessible from the shop floor and from a side alley staff entrance with a coded keypad.

suspects.name: Eileen
suspects.alibi: I was at my bench all morning working on a trouser hem. I did step into the alley around noon to take a personal call — maybe ten minutes — then came straight back.

suspects.name: Oscar
suspects.alibi: I was on the shop floor helping customers from when we opened until closing. I didn't leave the floor once. Ask any of them.

suspects.name: Dymphna
suspects.alibi: I was at the counter all morning. I stepped out at 1:15 to pick up a lunch order — the sandwich place is three minutes away — and was back by 1:35.

suspects.name: Kingsley
suspects.alibi: I came in around 11:40 to drop off two cleaned items and pick up one for same-day pressing. Signed the log, grabbed the overcoat, and left. I was in the back room two minutes, maybe three.

suspects.name: Rowena
suspects.alibi: I came in at 1:00 to check on my order. Dymphna wasn't at the counter so Oscar helped me. I didn't go into the back room at all.

starter: The neighboring property's building-mounted alley camera — shared access granted to police — covers the staff entrance. It shows: Eileen stepping out at 12:02 and re-entering at 12:11; Kingsley entering through the staff door at 11:38 carrying a two-item dry-cleaning bag, and leaving at 11:44 carrying what appears to be a three-item dry-cleaning bag. No other person enters via the alley during the theft window.

The courier log in the back room records each pickup and drop-off by ticket number. Kingsley's 11:40 entry lists two items dropped off (tickets 7714, 7715) and one item picked up (ticket 7716 — a customer's overcoat for same-day pressing), with his signature. Ticket 7716's hanging tag was found on the floor behind the finished-orders rack after the theft was reported — not on the overcoat, and not on the outgoing hook where it should have ended up.

The dry-cleaning firm Kingsley works for, Aldridge Pressing, has an active civil dispute with Lord Ashworth's estate over an unpaid commercial laundry contract for a hotel in his portfolio. The dispute was filed six weeks prior to the theft. A search of Kingsley's delivery van, authorized under the premises warrant, found the dinner jacket wrapped in a Aldridge Pressing protective cover in the cargo compartment.

exonerations.eileen: The shop floor camera covers the door to the back room. After Eileen returns from the alley at 12:11, she goes directly to her bench and does not enter the back room at any point for the remainder of the day. Her bench position is on the opposite side of the shop floor from the back room entrance.
exonerations.oscar: The shop floor camera confirms Oscar in the sales area continuously from opening to close. He assists Rowena at the counter at 1:00 PM. He does not enter the back room during any part of the theft window.
exonerations.dymphna: The front-door bell log records a chime at each door opening. Dymphna's lunch departure is logged at 1:16 PM and her return at 1:33 PM. The discovery of the missing jacket occurs only after Lord Ashworth's secretary phones at 12:55 to confirm collection — a call Dymphna took and noted before she left for lunch.
exonerations.rowena: Rowena enters the shop at 1:02 PM per the door log, is assisted at the counter by Oscar, and departs at 1:12 PM. The shop floor camera confirms she remained at the counter and viewing area throughout. She did not enter the back room.

reveal: The alley camera makes the case in one image: Kingsley enters with two items and leaves with three, while his own courier log records only one outgoing pickup. The third item in his bag is the dinner jacket — removed from the finished-orders rack and folded in before he walked out. Clue 2 sharpens the detail: the 7716 hanging tag dropped behind the rack shows exactly where he stood when he took the jacket, and a correctly processed pickup leaves the tag on the outgoing hook, not the floor. Clue 3 completes the picture with motive and physical evidence: Aldridge Pressing's active dispute with Lord Ashworth gives Kingsley a direct incentive tied to his employer, and the jacket itself was found in his van under an Aldridge cover. Eileen's bench camera, Oscar's floor coverage, Dymphna's door log, and Rowena's counter visit account for every other suspect.

title: The Empty Mount
case: At Delacroix Fine Jewellers on Mercer Lane, a one-of-a-kind rose-cut sapphire brooch appraised at £28,000 and awaiting client collection was removed from Display Case 3 in the private viewing room between 2:45 PM and 3:15 PM on a Thursday. The viewing room is accessible only through the main showroom and has no exterior window.

suspects.name: Sylvie
suspects.alibi: I was in the workshop all afternoon. The connecting door was open — I would have heard anything unusual from the viewing room. I never went in.

suspects.name: Benedict
suspects.alibi: I was on the showroom floor all day. I showed two appointments through the viewing room — one at 11 AM and one at 3:15 PM. At 3:15 I noticed the case was empty.

suspects.name: Margaux
suspects.alibi: I was at the viewing table completing a written appraisal from 1:30 to 2:45. I had the brooch out of the case for about 20 minutes to examine it. When I finished, I locked it back in the case and left at 2:53.

suspects.name: Finn
suspects.alibi: I came in at 2:10 to deliver a repaired bracelet from Delacroix's annex workshop. Handed it to Benedict at the counter and left — maybe four minutes total.

suspects.name: Claude
suspects.alibi: I had a 3:15 appointment. Benedict showed me several pieces, but he said the sapphire brooch wasn't available for viewing. I left at 3:40 and didn't touch any cases.

starter: The viewing room has a fixed overhead camera covering the display cases and the appraisal table. Margaux removes the brooch from Case 3 at 1:52 PM for examination and returns it at 2:43 PM. She closes the case at 2:45 PM. At 2:47 PM — two minutes after closing — she reopens Case 3 and reaches into it with her left hand. No other person enters the viewing room between 2:47 PM and Benedict's 3:15 PM arrival, at which point the case is empty.

At the time Margaux was contracted for the appraisal, she provided a professional reference from the Galton Institute of Gemology confirming her active membership. The Institute's member directory lists her primary employer as Hensley & Rue — a competing jeweller located 60 meters from Delacroix on the same street. Hensley & Rue's internal sourcing records, subpoenaed during the investigation, show an open commission for a Victorian rose-cut sapphire brooch marked as a priority request.

Margaux's professional appraisal case was not inspected on entry, as per Delacroix's standing courtesy policy for credentialed appraisers. The building's exit camera reviewed after the theft shows Margaux departing at 2:53 PM carrying a noticeably fuller bag profile than on her arrival at 1:28 PM, visible in the side-frame silhouette. A legal search of her vehicle found a padded jewelry transport pouch in a false-bottomed compartment; the pouch contained lint consistent in color and fiber type with the velvet lining of Case 3.

exonerations.sylvie: Sylvie was at her workshop bench from 1:15 PM through close of business, confirmed by the workshop's bench camera. The connecting door was positioned so the bench is not within eyeline of Case 3. She did not enter the viewing room at any point during the afternoon.
exonerations.benedict: The showroom camera places Benedict at the sales counter or with clients continuously throughout the afternoon. He entered the viewing room only at 3:15 PM for the scheduled appointment — after the brooch was already gone.
exonerations.finn: Finn's entry at 2:10 PM is recorded on the showroom camera. He approaches the counter, completes the delivery handoff with Benedict, and exits at 2:14 PM. He does not enter the viewing room; the viewing room camera confirms no entry during that window.
exonerations.claude_v: The client arrived at 3:15 PM and was accompanied by Benedict throughout the viewing room visit. The viewing room camera confirms the client stayed at the table and did not approach Case 3 unaccompanied. He departed at 3:40 PM.

reveal: The viewing room camera isolates the moment precisely: Margaux closes Case 3 at 2:45 PM, having completed her examination and written notes, and reopens it at 2:47 PM — a reopening with no legitimate appraisal purpose. No one else enters the room between that moment and Benedict's 3:15 arrival. The second opening is the theft. Clue 2 establishes motive and employer conflict: Margaux's primary employment at Hensley & Rue, 60 meters away, and their active priority commission for a Victorian rose-cut sapphire brooch make the purpose of the theft explicit — she came under the cover of a credentialed appraisal and identified the piece in advance. Clue 3 closes the physical chain: the fuller bag on exit, the padded pouch in her vehicle, and the Case 3 velvet lint confirm the brooch moved with her when she left at 2:53. Sylvie's bench camera, Benedict's showroom record, Finn's four-minute delivery, and the client's accompanied visit eliminate every other suspect.

title: The Guard Stance
case: At the Aldgate Fencing Club, a 19th-century French épée on loan from the Granger-Hollis estate for the club's centenary display was taken from its wall-mounted case in the trophy corridor between 5:30 PM and 7:00 PM during a Friday evening practice session. The corridor is unmonitored during practice and connects the changing rooms to the main salle.

suspects.name: Imogen
suspects.alibi: I was in my office doing end-of-month paperwork until about 6:45. My office door faces the corridor — I'd have heard anyone running or causing a commotion.

suspects.name: Felix
suspects.alibi: I was running the Friday intermediate session in the main salle from 5:30 to 7:00 sharp. Twelve fencers, non-stop drilling. I did not leave the floor once.

suspects.name: Willa
suspects.alibi: I finished a private lesson at 5:15 and stayed in the side salle to stretch and do footwork drills until around 6:30. I walked through the trophy corridor a couple of times going back and forth.

suspects.name: Damian
suspects.alibi: I was in the equipment room rewiring a scoring machine all evening. It's off the back of the main salle — I was there the whole time.

suspects.name: Rosa
suspects.alibi: I was registered for the intermediate session. I arrived at 5:25, changed, did the full session with Felix, then changed and left at 7:05.

starter: The trophy corridor has a motion-activated overhead light that logs each activation. Between 5:30 PM and 7:00 PM the light activated 7 times: 5:26 PM, 5:33 PM, 5:58 PM, 6:12 PM, 6:31 PM, 6:44 PM, and 6:48 PM. The wall-mounted display case has a tamper sensor that triggered at 6:12 PM. Willa's fob access record shows she entered the side salle at 5:20 PM and exited at 6:33 PM — placing her in the building and mobile during the 6:12 trigger.

The display case uses a push-latch mechanism that requires deliberate manipulation rather than accidental contact. The club's logbook records the case was last opened for display maintenance on Monday by Imogen and Damian together, per two-person protocol. A forensic inspection of the case's velvet interior found a faint pressure mark consistent with the seam pattern of a three-seam training glove — the type supplied in the club's communal equipment room — rather than the single-seam personal gloves typically used by advanced or visiting members.

Willa keeps personal equipment in a locker in the women's changing room. As part of a general premises inspection, her locker was opened with her consent. Inside her canvas equipment bag, a sealed side pocket contained a long narrow hardcase measuring 92 centimeters — consistent with the épée's 90-centimeter blade length. Willa said it held an old practice blade she planned to take for repointing. When the case was opened, it was empty.

exonerations.imogen: Imogen made a logged outgoing call from the club's landline at 5:55 PM and a second at 6:40 PM, placing her at her office desk during both windows. Her fob record shows no corridor zone entry after 5:20 PM. The tamper sensor triggered at 6:12 — between her two logged calls — with no exit interval on record.
exonerations.felix: Felix was in the main salle running the intermediate session from 5:30 to 7:00 PM, confirmed by twelve fencers and the salle's automatic session recording camera. He did not leave the floor during this period.
exonerations.damian: The equipment room has an internal camera used to monitor wiring work for liability purposes. It shows Damian at the scoring machine from 5:15 PM through 7:10 PM without interruption. The equipment room's entrance is off the main salle, with no direct access to the trophy corridor.
exonerations.rosa: Rosa is accounted for in the main salle from her 5:26 corridor entry through the session's end, confirmed by the salle recording. She exited through the corridor at 7:05 PM, corresponding to the final motion log activation.

reveal: The tamper sensor timestamps the theft at 6:12 PM. The corridor motion log shows an activation at 6:12, and Willa's fob places her in the building and free-moving during that window — the only suspect not continuously fixed elsewhere. Felix was on the salle floor on camera, Imogen was confirmed at her desk between two phone logs, Damian was on the equipment room camera, and Rosa was in the session. Clue 2 identifies the thief by the glove mark: a three-seam training glove was used to manipulate the latch, not the personal gloves of an advanced or visiting fencer. As a long-standing member, Willa had routine access to the club's communal training gloves in the equipment room — she would have used one precisely to avoid leaving a personal-glove print. Clue 3 finishes the case: a 92-centimeter hardcase in her locked equipment bag, dimensionally matching the stolen épée and empty when opened, has no innocent explanation.

title: The Missing Survey
case: At the Aldwick Cartographic Institute, a hand-colored 17th-century coastal survey on loan from the Brentwood Foundation was removed from its acid-free sleeve in the Rare Holdings vault sometime between 10:00 AM and 4:30 PM on a Tuesday. The vault uses a keypad entry log; a ceiling camera covers the central reading table but not the shelving alcoves along the north wall.

suspects.name: Hartley
suspects.alibi: I was at my desk or in meetings all day. I entered the vault once at 9:50 to prepare the table for the visiting scholar and left before he arrived.

suspects.name: Priscilla
suspects.alibi: I was in the conservation lab treating a 19th-century atlas all day. I had no reason to go near the Rare Holdings vault.

suspects.name: Emeric
suspects.alibi: I worked at the table until about 2:30, examining the coastal survey and making notes. I returned the map to the table before I left and handed my exit form to the counter.

suspects.name: Sable
suspects.alibi: I was doing maintenance rounds in the morning and dealing with a broken HVAC duct in the west wing from noon onward. I walked through the vault corridor once around 11 AM but didn’t enter.

suspects.name: Jonas
suspects.alibi: I was digitising maps in the scanning room all morning and cataloguing in the open stacks all afternoon. I had no reason to go near the vault.

starter: The vault’s keypad log records every entry and exit with badge ID and timestamp. Between 10:00 AM and 4:30 PM the vault was accessed four times: Hartley at 9:50 AM (pre-session setup, exited 10:02); Emeric at 10:15 AM (session start); Emeric again at 10:17 AM — a second entry logged 47 seconds after his first exit, attributed to a forgotten pen; and Emeric at 2:28 PM (logged session end). The ceiling camera covers the central table; the shelving alcoves along the north wall lie outside its field of view. No entry is recorded between Emeric’s 2:28 PM exit and Hartley’s 4:30 PM close-of-day check, at which point the acid-free sleeve was found to contain a period-style facsimile rather than the original.

Emeric’s access application cited an ongoing comparative study of 17th-century coastal triangulation methods. A search of academic databases shows the only matching publication — a monograph from his own department — was submitted for peer review two months before his application to the Institute was filed. The monograph’s bibliography does not cite the Brentwood survey. The Foundation’s loan officer confirmed the loan was approved solely on the strength of Emeric’s application, which described active research that had already concluded at the time of writing.

Camera footage shows Emeric at the table from 10:17 AM to approximately 2:03 PM. At 2:03 PM he carries the map to the north alcoves, citing a notation on the lower margin; he remains off-camera in the alcoves for eleven minutes and returns to the table at 2:14 PM with a map in hand before departing at 2:28 PM. The facsimile found in the sleeve was printed on aged-treatment paper requiring specialist preparation — not something producible on short notice. A Canon large-format pigment printer of the appropriate model was found in Emeric’s university office, and a warrant search of his vehicle recovered a flat archival transport case matching the Institute’s own vault-use type, with a barcode label matching the Brentwood survey’s accession number.

exonerations.hartley: Hartley’s vault entry was logged at 9:50 AM with an exit at 10:02 AM, eighteen minutes before Emeric arrived. Her keycard shows no further vault access until 4:30 PM. The table camera confirms the survey was in place when Emeric arrived.
exonerations.priscilla: Conservation lab sign-in records and the bench camera place Priscilla at her workstation from 8:45 AM through close of business without interruption. She did not access the vault keypad at any point that day.
exonerations.sable: Facilities maintenance logs and a contractor co-signature confirm Sable was working on the HVAC duct in the west wing from 11:50 AM through 3:15 PM. Her proximity badge shows no vault corridor scan during the relevant window.
exonerations.jonas: The scanning room requires a separate keycard swipe; Jonas’s log shows continuous check-ins from 8:30 AM through noon and open-stacks badge swipes from 1:10 PM onward. He did not access the vault corridor at any point that day.

reveal: The vault log isolates the critical anomaly: Emeric’s 47-second re-entry at 10:17 AM — attributed to a forgotten pen — was the moment he placed the prepared facsimile in the alcoves for a later swap. The camera’s blind spot in the shelving alcoves was not accidental; any researcher who had visited the Institute before — as Emeric had, at a public event two years earlier — could identify it. At 2:03 PM, with his session drawing to a close, he carried the original into the alcoves, exchanged it for the facsimile, and departed at 2:28 PM with the original inside his document case. Clue 2 establishes that the access application was fraudulent: the comparative study was already complete, making the entire visit a pretext. The facsimile’s specialist aged-treatment paper and the large-format printer in Emeric’s office confirm the substitution was planned weeks in advance. Clue 3 closes the physical chain: the archival transport case in his vehicle bearing the survey’s own accession barcode is not consistent with innocent scholarship. Hartley, Priscilla, Sable, and Jonas are each eliminated by objective electronic records placing them outside the vault throughout the relevant period.

title: The Darkroom Lift
case: At Aperture Works, a photography studio in the Eastmark arts quarter, a vintage Leica M3 camera body on long-term loan from a private collector — displayed on a shelf in the darkroom corridor as part of the studio’s heritage collection — was taken between 2:00 PM and 5:15 PM on a Thursday. The corridor has no camera; the studio uses keycard logs at its main entry and internal zone doors.

suspects.name: Margot
suspects.alibi: I was in my office or on the phone most of the afternoon. I walked through the corridor once around 3:30 to check the print drying racks and again at 5:15 when I noticed the M3 was gone.

suspects.name: Pierce
suspects.alibi: I had a client shoot in Studio B from 2:00 to 4:45. Soundproofed room, non-stop — I didn’t come out once.

suspects.name: Delia
suspects.alibi: I was in the darkroom developing a batch of film from 1:30 through about 4:00. I’d have noticed anyone coming past the corridor.

suspects.name: Cassius
suspects.alibi: I was in the rental annex cataloguing new stock most of the afternoon. I came through the corridor a few times to use the staff bathroom and check the print racks.

suspects.name: Nadia
suspects.alibi: I was in the workshop classroom from 2:00 to 5:00 for the evening printing session. I left once to get water from the kitchen around 3:00 and went straight back.

starter: The display shelf holding the M3 was fitted with a pressure sensor for insurance tracking; the sensor log shows the camera was removed at 3:41 PM. The studio’s zone keycard log records Cassius opening the storage annex — which connects via a staff pass-through to the darkroom corridor — at 3:38 PM, three minutes before the pressure sensor triggered. The darkroom door uses an airtight seal with a break sensor; the seal log shows no break between 1:30 PM and 4:07 PM, confirming Delia was sealed inside the darkroom during the theft window. No other keycard swipe places any staff member in the corridor zone between 3:30 and 3:45 PM except Cassius.

A review of Cassius’s phone records revealed a three-week text exchange with a contact saved as ‘H.F. Collectibles’ in the days before the theft. In it, Cassius describes a working Leica M3 at the studio, discusses its condition and serial number range, and receives a price estimate of €3,200–3,800. The final message, sent the evening before the theft, reads: ‘Confirmed for Thursday. Will have it by end of day.’ H.F. Collectibles is registered in Hamburg; cross-referencing import records links the contact to two previous transactions involving vintage Leica equipment.

The M3 was stored in its original brown leather ever-ready case. A search of the rental annex found the case’s detached strap stuffed behind a shelving unit — the strap had been cut from its attachment rings with a narrow blade. The attachment rings were in the annex workbench waste bin. Stripping the case from the body before shipping reduces traceability; the M3 body itself was not in the annex. A next-day international courier pickup filed in Cassius’s name was logged at 5:50 PM — 25 minutes before Margot discovered the camera missing.

exonerations.margot: Margot’s office phone records show an outgoing call at 2:45 PM lasting 31 minutes, placing her at her desk until 3:16 PM. Her corridor visit at 3:30 PM is her own account; no keycard log places her in the annex pass-through. She was not in the corridor zone during the 3:38–3:42 PM window.
exonerations.pierce: Studio B’s session recording system was active from 2:02 PM to 4:47 PM without interruption. The studio’s single door sensor shows no opening between 2:02 and 4:47 PM. Pierce did not exit during the theft window.
exonerations.delia: The darkroom door seal log registers no break between 1:30 PM and 4:07 PM. Delia could not have exited and re-entered without triggering the break sensor. She was physically inside the darkroom throughout the theft window.
exonerations.nadia: The workshop classroom uses an attendance scanner for insurance purposes. Nadia’s tag records a 2:03 PM entry and a 5:01 PM exit, with a single four-minute gap at 3:03 PM consistent with her kitchen account. She did not enter the darkroom corridor.

reveal: The pressure sensor pins the M3’s removal to 3:41 PM, and the annex keycard log places Cassius in the adjacent pass-through zone at 3:38 — the three-minute gap is exactly the time required to walk the corridor, lift the camera, and return. Delia’s sealed darkroom door eliminates the only other person with physical proximity to the corridor during that window. Clue 2 proves premeditation across three weeks: the text exchange with H.F. Collectibles, including the serial number discussion and the ‘Confirmed for Thursday’ message sent the night before, shows the theft was scheduled around the studio’s diary. Clue 3 provides the physical chain: the cut strap and attachment rings in the annex workbench area are the steps taken to separate a traceable case from a body before international shipment, and the courier pickup filed at 5:50 PM — before Margot even found the camera gone — was the attempt to move the piece across borders before any report could be made. Margot, Pierce, Delia, and Nadia are each cleared by records that place them away from the corridor zone during the three-minute window.

title: The Lost Movement
case: At Vanthorpe & Sons Horology, a working Swiss pocket watch movement dating to circa 1885 — brought in for service by estate client Mrs. Alderton — was found missing from its labelled service tray in the workshop at close of business on a Saturday. The movement had been removed from its case for cleaning and left in the tray from 11:00 AM onward. The workshop is separated from the public counter by an unlatched half-door; the shop’s single security camera covers the front counter area only.

suspects.name: Oswald
suspects.alibi: I was at the bench all day except lunch from 1:00 to 1:45 at the café across the street. I placed the movement in the service tray just before lunch.

suspects.name: Theodora
suspects.alibi: I came in at 2:15 to drop off a carriage clock for an estimate. I was in the front area the whole time. I never set foot in the workshop.

suspects.name: Rupert
suspects.alibi: I was at my bench in the back workshop all day. I didn’t touch anything on Oswald’s bench or in his service tray.

suspects.name: Nell
suspects.alibi: I was at the counter all day. I handled Theodora’s drop-off and two other walk-ins. I went into the workshop once around noon to ask Oswald a question about a repair.

suspects.name: Jerome
suspects.alibi: I came at 3:00 PM to deliver a parts order. Nell signed for it at the counter. I never went past the front area.

starter: The shop’s security camera covers the front counter and seating area. Theodora arrived at 2:15 PM and is visible on camera at the counter. At 2:23 PM, Nell steps into the back office to retrieve a pricing form, leaving the front area unattended for approximately three minutes. During this gap, Theodora is absent from both the counter and the seating area on camera — she reappears at 2:26 PM, returning to the counter just before Nell comes back. The workshop’s half-door was unlatched, as is routine during business hours. Rupert confirmed the service tray was present at 2:10 PM; Oswald found it empty at 5:30 PM. No other visitor entered the shop between 2:15 and 3:00 PM.

Theodora is a practicing estate auctioneer specialising in horology. Her firm’s online auction platform — Alderton Estate Clearance, active since March — lists a Patek Philippe-era pocket watch movement ‘awaiting reunification with its case’ as a forthcoming lot, with technical detail (keyless works, lever escapement, 20 jewels) matching the exact specification on the Vanthorpe service ticket. The estate client Mrs. Alderton is Theodora’s own auction client — the same Alderton name appears on both the service ticket and the auction platform. Theodora therefore knew the movement’s value, its caseless state, and its location before she arrived.

Officers searching Theodora’s vehicle found a small padded zipper pouch inside her coat on the rear seat. The pouch contained a bare pocket watch movement with no case. The serial number on the barrel bridge matched the Vanthorpe service ticket exactly. The pouch bore a partial fingerprint on the zipper pull matching Theodora’s right index finger. Inside the pouch was also the movement’s exhibition stand from the service tray — a small detail confirming the pouch had been prepared to hold the piece rather than carry it incidentally.

exonerations.oswald: Oswald placed the movement in the service tray just before his 1:00 PM lunch and returned at 1:45 PM. Rupert confirmed the tray was present at 2:10 PM — after Oswald’s return — establishing the movement was still in the workshop when Theodora arrived. Oswald was at his bench in Rupert’s sight from 1:50 PM onward with no unexplained gap.
exonerations.rupert: Rupert was at his back bench throughout the day and confirmed the tray’s presence at 2:10 PM — five minutes before Theodora arrived. His position at the back of the workshop, past Oswald’s bench, means he would have had to pass Oswald to reach the tray, which Oswald did not observe.
exonerations.nell: Nell’s three-minute absence from the front area at 2:23 PM was the window Theodora exploited. Nell’s camera record places her at the counter or in the back office throughout the afternoon; she was retrieving a pricing form during the gap, not in the workshop.
exonerations.jerome: Jerome’s delivery at 3:00 PM is confirmed by the front camera and Nell’s signed receipt at 3:02 PM. He did not pass beyond the counter area and departed at 3:04 PM — after the theft window had already closed.

reveal: The three-minute gap in Theodora’s camera presence at 2:23 PM — precisely coinciding with Nell’s absence from the front area — is the window of the theft. The half-door to the workshop was unlatched, as it routinely is; any visitor who knew the shop layout could walk through unobserved. Theodora, as an estate auctioneer who had consulted with Vanthorpe & Sons on prior valuations, was familiar with the premises. Clue 2 closes the motive with precision: the Alderton Estate auction listing describes the movement in technical terms matching the service ticket before the piece had been publicly advertised — meaning Theodora identified it as a target through her own estate work, then arranged a pretext visit to retrieve it while the movement was caseless and at its most vulnerable. The carriage clock drop-off was the pretext. Clue 3 ends the case: the movement in her coat, serial number matching the ticket, her fingerprint on the zipper, and the inclusion of the exhibition stand from the service tray confirm she took it with deliberate care, not accidentally. Oswald, Rupert, Nell, and Jerome are each cleared by presence accounts and camera records placing them away from the unmonitored workshop during Theodora’s three-minute off-camera window.

title: The Reserved Case
case: At CellarVault private wine storage in the Meridian Business District, a consignment of twelve bottles of 2005 Domaine de L’Église Pomerol — valued at approximately £4,800 and belonging to account holder Mr. Fenwick — was found missing from his climate-controlled storage cage during a quarterly audit on Monday morning. The cage uses a combination padlock whose code is held by the account holder and the facility manager. The cage showed no sign of forced entry.

suspects.name: Anselm
suspects.alibi: I manage the access codes and account records. I did the receiving check on Friday for another client’s delivery. I didn’t open Cage 14.

suspects.name: Cassiopeia
suspects.alibi: I was doing temperature calibration checks on a set of cages Saturday morning. Cage 14 wasn’t on my list — it was audited last month.

suspects.name: Mr. Fenwick
suspects.alibi: I haven’t visited the facility since I deposited the case in March. I gave the combination only to the facility for emergencies.

suspects.name: Dorset
suspects.alibi: I was on shift Friday night through Saturday morning. I do a walkthrough every two hours from the perimeter corridor. I didn’t see anyone in the cellar section.

suspects.name: Linh
suspects.alibi: I handled a Friday afternoon delivery for the Marchetti account. I was in the loading bay and front receiving area the whole time.

starter: The facility uses electronic proximity logs for the cellar entry door and a paper sign-in required by insurance policy for all cellar entries. Friday’s sign-in log records three entries: Cassiopeia at 9:15 AM (temperature round), Anselm at 2:05 PM (noted as ‘receiving coordination’), and Anselm again at 4:47 PM (noted as ‘end-of-day check’). Fenwick’s combination padlock was intact and correctly re-locked, ruling out forced entry. The facility’s master combination record — held in a sealed tamper-evident envelope in Anselm’s office safe — was found with its tamper seal broken. The insurance investigator assessed the adhesive oxidation as consistent with the envelope being opened within the past two weeks.

A civil court filing from eight months prior names Anselm as respondent in a £6,100 personal debt recovery action, currently in default. His bank statements show no payment toward the debt in the preceding four months. A transaction record from fine wine broker Hendricks & Mole shows a private seller placing twelve bottles of 2005 Domaine de L’Église Pomerol for sale in the same week as the missing case. The seller’s submitted description matched Fenwick’s lot number and bottle sequence precisely.

Forensic examination of the tamper-evident envelope recovered a full fingerprint from the inner lip matching Anselm’s right thumb, on record from his employment background check. The padlock on Cage 14 was intact and correctly locked — re-locked using the correct combination rather than forced. Separately, Cage 14’s secondary inventory card — a handwritten internal record kept in a folder on the cage door — had been updated to remove the Fenwick Pomerol line. The handwriting was assessed by a document examiner as consistent with Anselm’s writing samples taken from facility records.

exonerations.cassiopeia: Cassiopeia’s Saturday temperature round is logged electronically by her access card, with each of seventeen cage checks timestamped in sequence. Cage 14 does not appear in her round log. No gap in her sequence is long enough to access and re-lock Cage 14. Her Friday entry was at 9:15 AM, well before both of Anselm’s afternoon cellar visits.
exonerations.fenwick: Mr. Fenwick’s last facility access was in March, confirmed by the proximity log and a paper sign-in bearing his signature. He did not visit in the period before the audit. The combination was shared only with the facility manager for emergency use — not with any other staff.
exonerations.dorset: Dorset’s security walkthrough log, initialled at each perimeter station, is complete for Friday night and Saturday morning with no anomalies. His walkthroughs were conducted from the perimeter corridor, not the cellar interior. He is not listed in the cellar paper sign-in log for either day.
exonerations.linh: Linh’s Friday delivery is recorded in the receiving log from 2:30 PM to 4:10 PM. The loading bay camera shows her in the bay and front receiving area throughout. She signed out before Anselm’s 4:47 PM cellar entry and does not hold a combination to any storage cages.

reveal: Anselm held the two elements required for the theft: the combination to Cage 14 and unaccompanied access to the cellar. The facility’s master combination records are his responsibility, and the broken tamper-evident envelope with his fingerprint inside confirms he accessed Fenwick’s code before Friday.
