His two self-described routine visits on Friday afternoon — at 2:05 PM and 4:47 PM, both outside his normal duties for a delivery day — are the access windows, with the padlock re-locked using the correct combination and no sign of disturbance. Clue 2 establishes financial motive and closes the chain of custody: the Hendricks & Mole broker record describes a seller placing the exact lot in the same week, matching Fenwick’s bottle sequence. Clue 3 completes the case: his thumbprint inside the combination envelope, the correctly re-locked padlock requiring insider knowledge, and the updated inventory card in his own handwriting removing the missing case from the records show an attempt to make the bottles simply disappear from the books. Cassiopeia, Fenwick, Dorset, and Linh are each eliminated by logs and records that place them outside the relevant access window or without knowledge of the combination.

title: The Fired Piece
case: At Kiln & Form Gallery in the Westridge arts precinct, a signed stoneware vessel by ceramicist Ida Brömser — submitted for a group exhibition opening the following Saturday — was found missing from the gallery’s receiving storage room during a pre-installation check on Tuesday afternoon. The piece had been logged at intake on Friday and placed in the storage room with eleven other exhibition works.

suspects.name: Ida
suspects.alibi: I dropped the piece off on Friday afternoon and signed the intake form. I haven’t been back since.

suspects.name: Lewin
suspects.alibi: I was in the storage room doing condition checks from 9:00 AM to about 12:45, then went to lunch. I came back at 2:15 and noticed the Brömser was gone at 2:20.

suspects.name: Saffron
suspects.alibi: I was in my office or with a visiting journalist most of the day. I did a quick walkthrough at 11:30 but didn’t go into the storage room.

suspects.name: Otto
suspects.alibi: I came in at 9:00 AM to take wall measurements and left before noon. I went into the storage room once around 10:30 to check a larger piece’s dimensions.

suspects.name: Blythe
suspects.alibi: I was at the front desk all morning and helped hang a non-exhibition piece in the main gallery from 2:00 to 2:45. I didn’t go into the storage room.

starter: The storage room has a keycard door log and a condition check sheet requiring all handlers to initial against each piece. Tuesday’s sheet shows Lewin initialled all twelve exhibition pieces between 9:05 and 10:50 AM, including the Brömser at 10:14 AM. No further check entries were made. The door log shows Lewin’s keycard swiped at 12:47 PM — thirteen minutes before his claimed 1:00 PM lunch departure — with no corresponding condition check entry for that visit. The gallery car park validation system confirms his car departed at 1:03 PM and returned at 2:11 PM. The exterior camera shows him carrying a large canvas tote bag into the building at 2:11 PM; the same bag appears flat and empty in footage of his 9:00 AM arrival. Otto confirmed the Brömser was present at 10:30 AM; it was gone by 2:20 PM.

Lewin maintains a personal Instagram account documenting his interest in studio ceramics. A post from seven months ago shows him at an open studio event hosted by Ida Brömser with a comment: ‘asked about purchasing the untitled stoneware B series — she won’t sell direct.’ Three weeks before the exhibition intake he reposted a Brömser piece from a collector’s home with the caption ‘one day.’ A ceramics forum where Lewin is a registered user includes a thread from four months ago in which a user matching his handle asks whether a Brömser piece ‘acquired through unofficial channels’ could be listed through a third-party platform without provenance documentation.

When Lewin’s home was visited, investigators found the Brömser vessel wrapped in gallery-grade acid-free tissue — the same type stocked in the gallery’s storage room — inside a padded carrying case in his bedroom. The tissue bore a partial intake label from the gallery’s own labelling system, including the exhibition lot number assigned at Friday intake. A timestamped online order receipt in Lewin’s email confirmed the padded carrying case was purchased three days before the theft.

exonerations.ida: Ida’s last gallery access was at Friday intake, confirmed by the door log and the signed intake form. She has no keycard; the gallery’s door log shows no visitor entry consistent with her presence on Tuesday.
exonerations.saffron: Saffron’s 11:30 AM walkthrough is confirmed by the gallery’s internal camera and by the visiting journalist’s own account. Her office phone records show meetings from 10:00 AM through 12:45 PM. She did not enter the storage room.
exonerations.otto: Otto’s car park validation log confirms departure at 11:47 AM. The storage room keycard log shows no swipe in his name after 10:28 AM. He was not present during the 12:47 PM window.
exonerations.blythe: Interns are issued visitor badges that log main-entrance access only — Blythe does not hold a storage room keycard. Her visitor badge log records continuous front-desk check-ins. She could not have opened the storage room door.

reveal: The 12:47 PM keycard swipe is the pivot: it is Lewin’s only storage room entry not recorded on the condition check sheet, inserted thirteen minutes before his claimed lunch departure. The Brömser was present at 10:30 AM (confirmed by Otto) and gone by 2:20 PM, with no other keycarded staff accessing the room between Otto’s 10:28 AM exit and Lewin’s 12:47 PM swipe. The tote bag, empty on arrival and visibly loaded on his 2:11 PM return, marks the moment the piece left the building — in his lunch bag, not via a back door. Clue 2 establishes the obsession and the premeditation: the social media trail spanning seven months, the failed purchase request at an open studio, and the forum question about selling a piece ‘acquired through unofficial channels’ show Lewin had been planning to obtain a Brömser by non-standard means long before the exhibition was announced. Clue 3 closes the case: the vessel in his bedroom wrapped in the gallery’s own intake tissue with the lot number label still attached, stored in a carrying case ordered three days before the theft, is conclusive. Ida, Saffron, Otto, and Blythe are eliminated by access logs, departure records, or the absence of keycard rights.

title: The Night Count
case: At the Graysmore Club, a private members’ institution in the Belmore district, £3,200 in cash collected during the annual charity auction — sealed in a numbered deposit envelope and locked in the club’s main floor safe — was found to be £900 short when the treasurer conducted the morning count on Wednesday. The sealed envelope had been opened and resealed with a replacement tamper strip, and the original numbered seal was gone.

suspects.name: Cressida
suspects.alibi: I was behind the bar from 9 PM until close at 1 AM. The safe is in the office behind the bar, which I use to secure the register at end of night. I locked the register cash in around 12:55 AM.

suspects.name: Percival
suspects.alibi: I counted the auction proceeds at 9:45 PM, sealed the envelope, and locked it in the safe myself. I left the club at 10:30 PM.

suspects.name: Hadley
suspects.alibi: I ran the auction from 6:30 to 9:30 PM and handed the proceeds envelope to Percival at 9:40 PM. I left the club at 10:00 PM.

suspects.name: Russel
suspects.alibi: I was at the front door from 7 PM until close. I carried some auction items to the storage room around 10:30 and was back at the door by 10:45. I didn’t go near the office.

suspects.name: Dorinda
suspects.alibi: I came in at 1:15 AM after the club closed and cleaned until 5:00 AM. The office was already locked when I arrived.

starter: The club safe uses an LED open-counter log that increments on each opening. Tuesday night’s count shows six openings: Percival’s at 9:45 PM, Cressida’s register-lock entry at 12:55 AM, and four additional unexplained openings at 11:12 PM, 11:47 PM, 12:04 AM, and 12:31 AM. The bar’s security camera covers the area in front of the bar, not the office behind it. At 11:10 PM, 11:46 PM, 12:03 AM, and 12:30 AM, the bar camera shows the front area empty with no patrons present; Cressida is absent from camera during short gaps of two to four minutes at each of these times, each gap aligning with an unexplained safe entry.

The deposit envelope recovered from the safe was resealed with a TrustSeal silver tamper strip. The club’s own supply uses BancSafe gold strips exclusively — confirmed by a stock check of the club’s stationery cupboard. A box of TrustSeal silver strips was found in the locked drawer beneath the bar, to which only Cressida holds the key during her shifts. The drawer also contained £120 in mixed-denomination notes kept separately from the till, consistent in denomination breakdown with partial amounts removed from the auction envelope across multiple entries.

The original BancSafe gold seal was recovered from the waste bin behind the bar, pressed flat under a paper cocktail order. Forensic examination confirmed it had been opened with a sharp implement along one edge — a clean slice rather than a tear. A box cutter was found in Cressida’s service kit beneath the bar; the blade tested positive for trace adhesive consistent with BancSafe tamper-evident strip compound. The envelope had been sliced in the bar area and resealed in the office across four separate sessions, explaining the incremental shortfall and the multiple safe openings.

exonerations.percival: Percival’s safe entry at 9:45 PM is the first after Hadley’s handover and is confirmed by his own account and the counter log. He departed at 10:30 PM, confirmed by the front-door camera and Russel’s departure log. He did not return that night.
exonerations.hadley: Hadley’s departure at 10:00 PM is confirmed by the front-door camera and Russel’s visitor log. She did not hold the safe combination and was not in the building during any of the four unexplained openings.
exonerations.russel: Russel’s position at the front door is confirmed by the door camera and his member departure log. His storage room trip at 10:30 PM was captured on the corridor camera and lasted eleven minutes. He did not enter the office area at any point.
exonerations.dorinda: Dorinda arrived at 1:15 AM, after all four unexplained safe entries. Her sign-in with the outgoing night manager is timed at 1:18 AM. The office was already locked on her arrival, and she does not hold the safe combination.

reveal: The safe counter records six entries, of which only two — Percival’s at 9:45 PM and Cressida’s at 12:55 AM — are accounted for. The four unexplained entries at 11:12 PM, 11:47 PM, 12:04 AM, and 12:31 AM correspond precisely to four gaps in Cressida’s bar camera presence, each two to four minutes long and each at a moment when the bar was empty of patrons. The office is directly behind her station, the safe combination was available to her as closing manager on duty, and the unmonitored rear of the bar is the only zone not covered by camera. Clue 2 establishes premeditation: replacement TrustSeal strips in her locked bar drawer, not the club’s own BancSafe stock, prove she arrived prepared with the materials to reseal the envelope after taking cash in installments. Clue 3 ties the physical act to her station: the original gold seal — cleanly sliced with a sharp implement — was in the bar waste bin, and the box cutter blade in her service kit carries adhesive residue from the strip compound. Four separate entries, four small withdrawals, four fresh reseals: a method designed to keep the shortfall below the threshold that would trigger an immediate alarm. Percival, Hadley, Russel, and Dorinda are each eliminated by departure records, camera logs, and absence of safe combination access.

title: The Empty Plinth
case: At the Merfield Sports Complex, three gold-plated medals awarded at the club’s 1973 founding tournament — kept in a locked display case in the clubhouse corridor as the club’s founding artefacts — were found missing on Thursday morning. The display case uses a barrel key held by the club administrator. The medals had been confirmed in place during the previous evening’s club dinner, which ran from 7:00 PM to approximately 10:15 PM.

suspects.name: Gareth
suspects.alibi: I hold the display case key. I was at the dinner all evening and helped with the clear-up after, leaving around 10:45 PM. I passed the corridor on the way out and didn’t notice anything wrong.

suspects.name: Sylvia
suspects.alibi: I was at the dinner and left early, around 9:45 PM. I walked past the corridor display case on the way out.

suspects.name: Nate
suspects.alibi: I was doing pre-dinner setup from 5:00 to 7:00 PM and then back at 10:00 PM for the post-dinner clear. I was in the main hall and kitchen all evening.

suspects.name: Petra
suspects.alibi: I was at the dinner all evening and gave a short speech about the 1973 tournament at about 8:30 PM. I left with the last group at around 10:15 PM.

suspects.name: Callum
suspects.alibi: I was serving and clearing in the main hall from 6:30 PM through 10:30 PM. I went to the storage cupboard off the corridor once around 9:00 PM to fetch extra napkins.

starter: The corridor has a motion-activated light that logs each activation. Between 7:00 PM and 11:00 PM the light activated seven times: 7:04 PM (guests arriving), 8:55 PM (consistent with Callum’s napkin run), 10:08 PM and 10:16 PM (consistent with early-departing guests), 10:44 PM, 10:51 PM, and 10:58 PM. By 10:44 PM, Petra had left at 10:15, Sylvia at 9:45, and the catering CCTV confirms Callum signed out at 10:34 PM. Nate was in the main hall on camera until 11:10 PM. The three paired activations at 10:44, 10:51, and 10:58 PM are consistent with a single person making two trips between the corridor and a nearby room during clear-up. Gareth stated he left at 10:45 PM; his car is not recorded on the car park exit camera until 11:02 PM.

Financial records show Gareth in arrears on a £5,000 personal loan, with no payments in the preceding three months. A memorabilia auction listing posted at 8:47 AM Thursday — before the theft was reported to the committee at 9:30 AM — advertised ‘three vintage gold-plated athletics medals, 1970s founding tournament, club provenance.’ The listing was posted from an IP address resolving to the Merfield Sports Complex public Wi-Fi network, accessed by police warrant. The listing was deleted 28 minutes after officers arrived at the complex.

The display case barrel lock showed no sign of forced entry — it was opened with the correct key. No duplicate key exists, per club records: the barrel key has been on Gareth’s administrative key ring for seven years. A forensic examination of the key ring found a fleck of gold-tinted lacquer on the barrel key’s bow, consistent with the medals’ surface coating, transferred during recent use. The medals themselves were recovered from a padded pouch inside a locked drawer in Gareth’s office desk the following morning.

exonerations.sylvia: Sylvia departed at 9:45 PM, confirmed by two members who left with her and by the car park exit camera timestamping her vehicle at 9:48 PM. She was gone more than 55 minutes before the 10:44 PM corridor activation.
exonerations.nate: The main hall catering CCTV shows Nate in the hall or kitchen from 10:00 PM through 11:10 PM without interruption. The corridor motion log shows no activation consistent with his position during that window.
exonerations.petra: Petra departed with the last dinner group at approximately 10:15 PM, confirmed by the car park camera. Her departure predates the 10:44 PM activation by 29 minutes.
exonerations.callum: Callum’s 8:55 PM corridor visit corresponds to his napkin run. The catering CCTV places him in the hall or kitchen from 9:05 PM through 10:30 PM. He signed out on the catering contractor’s time sheet at 10:34 PM — ten minutes before the first unexplained corridor activation.

reveal: The corridor motion log places someone in the corridor at 10:44, 10:51, and 10:58 PM. By 10:44 PM every other suspect had either left the building or is fixed in the main hall on camera. Gareth’s stated 10:45 PM departure is contradicted by the car park exit camera, which does not record his vehicle until 11:02 PM — seventeen minutes he spent in the building after claiming to have left, during which all three unexplained activations occurred. Clue 2 provides motive and confirms premeditation: the memorabilia listing posted at 8:47 AM from the complex’s own Wi-Fi — before the theft was even reported — means the listing was drafted the previous evening and posted first thing the next morning, showing the sale was planned before the medals were taken. Clue 3 ends the argument: the display case was opened with the only existing barrel key, which has been on Gareth’s ring for seven years; lacquer from the medals’ surface is on the key from recent use; and the medals were in a locked drawer in his own office. Sylvia, Nate, Petra, and Callum are each cleared by departure logs, catering CCTV, or the corridor motion record.
